stories.title: Colleen Wenke of Taconic Partners: 5 Questions
stories.excerpt: If you thought Times Square was only a destination for international tourists, suburban out-of-towners and rowdy event-goers, think again. Taconic Partners is giving the busy New York City neighborhood a residential makeover with continued success at the Ellery, a 32-story, 330-unit luxury apartment tower at 312 West 43rd Street that opened two years ago and is now fully leased. The secret to attracting residents to Times Square? Creating a “calm oasis” within the chaos, according to Colleen Wenke, president and chief operating officer at Taconic. The Ellery’s 10,000-square-foot indoor garden and 40-foot-long pool overlooking the city — along with its close proximity to mass transit — make it an alluring attraction for New York City residents who want to live closer to work, Wenke said. Thinking outside the box and building residential properties in previously untouched neighborhoods like Times Square might also help create some much-needed housing in the city, she said. Commercial Observer sat down with Wenke earlier this month to discuss New York City’s emerging residential areas, as well as Taconic’s current projects and whether the company is pursuing more housing under current tax incentive programs. This interview has been edited for length and clarity. Commercial Observer: The Ellery, Taconic’s 32-story luxury apartment tower in Times Square, is fully leased. Why do you think Times Square is becoming more of a residential neighborhood? Colleen Wenke: That’s been in occupancy for about 18 months, so we’re in our first year of renewals there. I’m very proud and excited to say that our retention is quite strong, and I think that’s a testament to the product that was delivered. Our thought process about all of our multifamily and all of our product types across all asset types is really focused on the quality in what we’re delivering, whether it’s our office space, homes, lab and life science spaces. We think about who’s going
bullets: Taconic Partners' Ellery (330 units, Times Square) fully leased two years post-opening.
Indoor garden, pool, transit proximity drive luxury residential demand in previously avoided Manhattan neighborhoods.
Untouched neighborhoods like Times Square represent supply opportunity for NYC housing shortage.
stories.summary: Taconic Partners' 330-unit luxury tower at 312 West 43rd Street in Times Square reached full occupancy two years after opening by creating lifestyle amenities (10,000-sq-ft indoor garden, 40-foot pool) that position the building as a 'calm oasis' within the bustling neighborhood. The project demonstrates that mass transit access and thoughtful design can attract renters to previously undesirable Manhattan areas, suggesting underdeveloped residential submarkets remain viable for new supply.
stories.impact: Times Square's successful residential conversion signals untapped demand pockets in high-transit Manhattan neighborhoods previously abandoned by residential developers. This validates a development thesis for underutilized commercial or mixed-use corridors in major transit hubs—opportunities to acquire, rezone, and build ahead of neighborhood maturation, particularly in areas with strong foot traffic but limited housing stock.

stories.title: NYC’s top construction permits: Week ending June 12, 2026
stories.excerpt: 🏆 New Building: The largest new building application filed last week was in Brooklyn. The Gotham Organization plans to construct a 533,487-square-foot, three-building mixed-use development at 12040 Flatlands Avenue, 395 Louisiana Avenue and 12030 Flatlands Avenue in East New York. The buildings will stand between 12 and 14 stories tall and have a combined 599 housing units. Gloria Glas is the architect behind the project. Job Filing Numbers: B01412657-I1, B01412138-I1, B01412655-I1 📊 New Building: In Long Island City, ZD Jasper plans to build a 23-story, mixed-use building at 35-25 Steinway Street. It will measure 120,490 square feet and house 99 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: East New York (Brooklyn): 599-unit, 3-building development (533k SF) recently permitted.
Long Island City: 99-unit, 23-story mixed-use project filed; Greenpoint: 99-unit, 9-story mixed-use.
NYC pipeline: 697 residential units in three major permits alone in single week (June 12, 2026).
stories.summary: NYC's top construction permits for the week ending June 12, 2026 included a 599-unit mixed-use development in East New York (three 12–14 story buildings), a 99-unit project in Long Island City, and a 99-unit development in Greenpoint, totaling 697 new residential units across three major filings. The largest single project was The Gotham Organization's 533,487-sq-ft East New York development at 12040 Flatlands Avenue and adjacent parcels.
stories.impact: Strong residential permitting velocity in Brooklyn (East New York, Greenpoint) and Queens (Long Island City) indicates active pipeline in outer boroughs where zoning is more favorable and land costs lower than Manhattan. Developers are consolidating or planning large mixed-use assemblies in emerging submarkets; watch absorption and pre-leasing on these projects to gauge whether supply is outpacing demand in outer-borough corridors.

stories.title: The Plan: Suburb Meets City at 487 Henry Street in Brooklyn
stories.excerpt: You get the best of both worlds at this newly completed luxurious — yet unassuming — townhouse in Brooklyn’s tranquil Cobble Hill neighborhood. Situated along a quaint, tree-lined boulevard, 487 Henry Street aligns perfectly with the vibe and visual of the neighborhood, looking outwardly like any other Brooklyn brownstone. The inside tells a different story. 487 Henry Street is a 6,300-square-foot, $14.5 million oasis featuring five bedrooms, six bathrooms, and a 130-year-old American elm tree in the backyard. In all this, it offers buyers a taste of both the city and suburban life. “We are a developer that specializes in townhouses, and this is probably our 29th townhouse,” said Carlos Saavedra, founder — along with wife Nicole Eckstrom — and principal of development firm Eckstrom NYC. “We do townhouses, we do condominiums, and we do one-family homes. And this one, the vibe seemed like it would be a very good one-family home.” And what a home that family will have. You’d be hard-pressed to find a room at 487 Henry Street that you would want to leave. What’s Commercial Observer’s favorite room? So glad you asked: It would have to be the main bathroom, which features a free-standing, deep-soaking tub, a separate closed-door stall for the toilet, and a separate stall for the steam shower, which has a rainfall showerhead. The bathroom also has a mini fridge, a frequent request among buyers, and not just for cosmetics and skin care. “Ozempic needs to be refrigerated,” Saavedra said. “It’s so much more common these days than it used to be, people have become more open about it, and buyers are asking for this.” Renovations at 487 Henry Street were completed in March, and the property was listed in April. Built between 1844 and 1845, the property now spans six levels and features two entrances, one on the first floor and one on the lower level. There are several outdoor spaces, including a glorious backyard, a small terrace off the main bedroom, and a rooftop lounge comple
bullets: 487 Henry Street, Cobble Hill: $14.5M, 6,300-SF renovated townhouse with suburban/urban hybrid appeal.
Developer Carlos Saavedra's firm specializes in townhouse conversion (29th property); targets mixed lifestyle positioning.
Single-family/small multifamily conversion trend in Brooklyn's historic neighborhoods.
stories.summary: Saavedra's development firm, which specializes in townhouse renovations, completed a $14.5 million repositioning of 487 Henry Street in Cobble Hill, Brooklyn—a 6,300-sq-ft, five-bedroom property marketed as offering 'both city and suburban life.' The property, sold in April after March completion, exemplifies the developer's strategy of high-end single-property conversions in established Brooklyn neighborhoods.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact to multifamily development. This represents single-family/small luxury house repositioning, not apartment supply. However, the narrative reflects strong buyer demand for lifestyle-hybrid properties in desirable Brooklyn neighborhoods—a signal that demographic segments seeking 'suburban amenities in urban settings' are willing to pay premium prices, which could inform mixed-use or ground-floor amenity design in adjacent multifamily development.

stories.title: Inside the AI Revolution Quietly Reshaping CRE’s Slowest Data Layer
stories.excerpt: Most commercial real estate decisions begin with the same question: What can I do with this land? It is the most important question in the industry. Until recently, it was also one of the slowest to answer. Zoning rules are fragmented across more than 25,000 jurisdictions, buried in legal code, and constantly amended. A single-line query could turn into a three-day research project. Last week, on Crexi, the marketplace where hundreds of thousands of brokers, developers, and investors evaluate properties, the answer started arriving in seconds. Crexi integrated Zoneomics, an AI-enabled zoning data platform covering more than 25,000 U.S. cities, directly into its analytics product. Understanding what can be built on a site has traditionally meant navigating municipal databases that vary in quality, format, and accessibility across thousands of jurisdictions. The integration eliminates that detour. With Zoneomics integrated into Crexi Intelligence, users now access zoning classifications, permitted uses, and parcel boundary geometry directly on a property’s detail page. They can pose plain-language queries such as “Can I build a multifamily here?” or “Is manufacturing permitted on this site?” and receive direct, source-backed answers drawn from the underlying data. Interactive map overlays and filtering extend the same capability to market-level analysis, surfacing redevelopment opportunities by permitted use without leaving the platform. The launch arrives at a moment when zoning has never mattered more. According to the Housing Affordability Institute, state legislatures introduced 412 housing-reform bills in 2025 and enacted 124 of them. Office-to-apartment conversions reached 90,300 announced units nationally at the start of 2026, a 28 percent year-over-year jump. Proptech venture capital reached $16.7 billion globally in 2025, a 67.9 percent year-over-year increase, according to CRETI. Taken together, the signals point to an industry
bullets: Zoneomics AI zoning platform integrated into Crexi Intelligence; covers 25,000+ US jurisdictions.
Plain-language queries now answer 'Can I build multifamily here?' in seconds instead of 3+ days.
Eliminates fragmented municipal database research bottleneck across 25,000 zoning jurisdictions.
stories.summary: Crexi has integrated Zoneomics, an AI-enabled zoning data platform covering 25,000+ U.S. cities, directly into its analytics product. Users can now submit plain-language queries (e.g., 'Can I build multifamily here?') and receive instant zoning classifications, permitted uses, and parcel geometry data directly on property detail pages, eliminating the traditional three-day manual research process required to navigate fragmented municipal databases.
stories.impact: Material efficiency gain for site reconnaissance and deal sourcing across all multifamily developers. Faster zoning clarity reduces pre-acquisition due diligence cost and cycle time, enabling faster scoring of land deals and opportunities in markets with favorable entitlements. This advantage accrues to developers with Crexi platform access and scales site screening efficiency—expect increased competition for shovel-ready or easily-entitleable land as the research bottleneck dissolves.

stories.title: Trump’s Federal Building Selloff Runs Into $26B Repair Backlog
stories.excerpt: Donald Trump’s plan to shrink the federal government’s real estate footprint has run into an 11-figure obstacle: The buildings need a lot of work. The U.S. General Services Administration (GSA) has identified more than $25.8 billion in maintenance needs across federally owned buildings, including at least 62 properties that each require $100 million or more in repairs, Bloomberg reported. Meanwhile, the Public Buildings Reform Board estimates the federal backlog could exceed $50 billion. The bottleneck is complicating the Trump Administration’s push to consolidate federal office space and sell off underutilized government property. General Services Administration Administrator Ed Forst listens during a meeting in April. PHOTO: Al Drago/Getty Images GSA Administrator Ed Forst, whom President Trump tapped to oversee the federal landlord agency, also said the plan is being slowed by congressional approval rules. Any repair project costing roughly $4 million or more must clear a so-called prospectus threshold, a process that can take more than a year before the work can be bid out. The issue has become more pressing while many agencies still occupy outdated buildings. Earlier in Trump’s second term, during the height of Elon Musk’s Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) push, the GSA briefly posted a list of 443 federal buildings it was considering for sale, then pulled the list the following day. Forst said the agency is now trying to take a more deliberate approach. But he argued that consolidating federal offices and selling excess property often requires the government to first invest in the buildings it wants to keep. Even GSA’s headquarters has become an example, with 40 percent of the building deemed as unusable. GSA data show that more than $1 billion in repairs went uncompleted in fiscal year 2025 because proposals were not approved before the fiscal year ended. The Jacob K. Javits Federal Office Building in New York City needs $410 million in repairs, acco
bullets: Federal building repair backlog: $25.8B identified (GSA); Public Buildings Reform Board estimates $50B+.
62+ federally owned properties each require $100M+ in repairs; prospectus approval process delays >1 year.
Trump administration selloff plans stalled by deferred maintenance and congressional approval thresholds.
stories.summary: The U.S. General Services Administration has identified $25.8 billion in maintenance needs across federally owned buildings, with the Public Buildings Reform Board estimating the total backlog could exceed $50 billion. At least 62 properties individually require $100 million or more in repairs. The Trump administration's plan to consolidate federal office space and sell underutilized properties is being slowed by repair backlogs and congressional prospectus approval requirements (projects costing ~$4 million+ require >1 year approval).
stories.impact: Limited direct impact to multifamily development. However, large-scale federal property dispositions could eventually flood secondary real estate markets with distressed or deferred-maintenance buildings, potentially creating acquisition and conversion opportunities for developers in gateway cities (if adaptive-reuse-to-multifamily becomes viable). Monitor GSA auction schedules in major metros; deep discounts on trophy federal buildings could unlock ground-floor commercial or mixed-use redevelopment opportunities adjacent to new residential.

stories.title: Delshah Capital Buys 227 and 456 Grand Street in Williamsburg for $85M
stories.excerpt: DelShah Capital has purchased two mixed-use residential properties in Williamsburg, Brooklyn. The Michael Shah-led firm bought 227 Grand Street and 456 Grand Street — the latter of which comes with a 421-a tax abatement that doesn’t expire until 2030 — for a combined $85 million from Bronstein Properties, The Real Deal reported. Bronstein paid $43 million for 456 Grand in 2018, according to property records. 456 Grand, which has 52 apartments, two retail spots and 50 parking spaces, sold for $39.5 million, while 227 Grand, which has 41 apartments, 10 retail slots and 21 spaces for parking, sold for $45.5 million, according to TRD. The two properties are half a mile apart, but separated by the Brooklyn-Queens Expressway and the onramps to the Williamsburg Bridge. Delshah and Bronstein did not immediately respond to requests for comment. The purchase was financed with a $60.5 million loan from Prospect Ridge. It was unclear what the buyer’s plans for the property are. Prospect Ridge did not immediately respond to a request for comment. The building at 456 Grand was constructed in 2015 under New York City’s PlaNYC Brownfield Cleanup Program. Bronstein acquired 227 Grand in a 2022 bankruptcy sale. Mark Hallum can be reached at mhallum@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Delshah Capital buys 227 & 456 Grand Street, Williamsburg: $85M combined ($39.5M + $45.5M).
456 Grand: 52 units, 421-a tax abatement through 2030; 227 Grand: 41 units, 10 retail, 21 parking.
Financed with $60.5M Prospect Ridge loan; buyer plans unclear.
stories.summary: Delshah Capital acquired two mixed-use residential properties in Williamsburg, Brooklyn—227 Grand Street (41 apartments, 10 retail, 21 parking) for $45.5 million and 456 Grand Street (52 apartments, 2 retail, 50 parking with 421-a abatement through 2030) for $39.5 million—for a combined $85 million from Bronstein Properties. Prospect Ridge financed the purchase with a $60.5 million loan; Delshah's post-acquisition strategy was not disclosed.
stories.impact: Williamsburg acquisition activity signals continued investor appetite for outer-Brooklyn multifamily with tax abatement benefit (456 Grand's 421-a extends to 2030, providing 4–5 years of additional shelter). The $85M buy-in for 93 units (~$914k per unit) in a maturing submarket suggests either value-add thesis (repositioning/rent growth) or hold-for-cashflow with tax subsidy benefit. Watch for refinancing or disposition announcements to understand Delshah's capital cycle intent; early acquisition patterns in Williamsburg may indicate broader investor positioning ahead of tax abatement expirations.

stories.title: SFA Agency Extends, Expands Tenancy at One Battery Park Plaza
stories.excerpt: Rudin said Tuesday that current tenant SFA Agency LLC will grow its footprint at One Battery Park Plaza, a 35-story, 800,000- square-foot office tower in Lower Manhattan. SFA Agency, an affiliate of Northwestern Mutual, will now occupy 14,071 square feet in a 10-year lease extension. A tenant at One Battery Park Plaza since 2023, the financial services company will expand by 5,170 square feet and relocate in early 2027. “SFA Agency’s long-term extension and expansion is a direct reflection of our commitment to superior customer service,” said Kevin Daly, VP of office leasing at Rudin. “As evidenced by the leasing success this year and reaching virtual full occupancy at One Battery Park Plaza, there remains a strong and growing market appetite for well-located and high-quality office space in Lower Manhattan.” Daly represented building ownership in the transaction. Scott Vinett of Savills represented SFA Agency. One Battery Park is now 99% leased. The post SFA Agency Extends, Expands Tenancy at One Battery Park Plaza appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: SFA Agency (Northwestern Mutual affiliate) expands at One Battery Park Plaza (Lower Manhattan office).
10-year lease extension; 5,170 SF expansion; building now 99% leased, early 2027 occupancy.
Office tower stabilization signal amid NYC office market volatility.
stories.summary: SFA Agency LLC, a financial services affiliate of Northwestern Mutual, has executed a 10-year lease extension and 5,170-sq-ft expansion at One Battery Park Plaza, a 35-story, 800,000-sq-ft office tower in Lower Manhattan. The expansion and relocation will occur in early 2027. One Battery Park Plaza is now 99% leased, demonstrating strong market appetite for high-quality office space in Lower Manhattan despite broader sector headwinds.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact to multifamily development. This is an office transaction. However, office stabilization in Lower Manhattan (a supply-constrained, transit-rich submarket) may reduce conversion pressure on Class-B office-to-multifamily redevelopment in that immediate area. Continued office demand preservation in Lower Manhattan means fewer redevelopment opportunities for residential developers, though it also stabilizes the district's daytime population and reinforces transit/walkability that supports nearby residential.

stories.title: DTLA’s Bank of America Plaza Trades for $210M in Receivership Sale
stories.excerpt: (Updated with additional information) Colliers on Tuesday announced the sale of 333 S. Hope St., a/k/a Bank of America Plaza, in Downtown Los Angeles. The 1.4-million-square-foot office tower traded through a receivership sale, with fee-simple title delivered at closing. The asset traded for $210 million, making it the largest office sale in Los Angeles in 2026 and the largest in Los Angeles County since 2023, according to Colliers. Vice chair Sean Fulp, EVPs Mark Schuessler and Todd Tydlaska, and associate VPs Jordan Garcia and Blake Hammerstein of Colliers’ investment sales team marketed the property and facilitated the transaction on behalf of the court-appointed receiver, Chris Nielsen, managing partner and co-head of Trigild. The buyer, Capital Group, was represented by Kevin Bender and Andrew Harper of JLL. “The sale is a meaningful step forward in the evolution of Downtown Los Angeles and another benchmark transaction for our team,” said Schuessler. “In conjunction with the receiver and special servicer, we were able to deliver pricing and deal terms that exceeded expectations, without a need for seller-financing.” Established in 1931, Capital Group has been a tenant at Bank of America Plaza since 1978. Post-acquisition, the property will 333 remain a multi-tenant building, with Capital Group as the largest and anchor tenant. The investment firm expects to add five floors to its current 14-floor occupancy when all locally based associates, who currently work across three locations, are brought in. “Completing the purchase of 333 S. Hope St. reinforces our long-term commitment to our associates, our clients, and our presence in Los Angeles,” said Rob Klausner, COO of Capital Group. “This building has been our home for decades, and as both landlord and anchor tenant, we are making meaningful investments to enhance the workplace experience today while building for the decades ahead.” The post DTLA’s Bank of America Plaza Trades for
bullets: Bank of America Plaza, DTLA: $210M sale in receivership (largest LA office sale since 2023).
Capital Group buyer (long-time tenant since 1978); consolidating 3-location workforce into 14 + 5 floors.
Anchor tenant anchors stabilization; office market floor-finding in DTLA.
stories.summary: Bank of America Plaza (333 S. Hope St.), a 1.4-million-sq-ft office tower in Downtown Los Angeles, traded for $210 million in a receivership sale—the largest office transaction in LA in 2026 and LA County since 2023. Capital Group, which has been a tenant since 1978, purchased the property as fee-simple and plans to consolidate local associates from three locations, expanding from 14 floors to 19 floors.
stories.impact: Modest impact. Capital Group's consolidation and expansion (14 → 19 floors) signals strong anchor-tenant demand in DTLA, reducing office supply and conversion risk for adjacent or competing office properties. However, no direct link to multifamily development. Monitor for second-order effects: if Capital Group's commitment stabilizes DTLA office market, it reduces urgency for Class-B office-to-multifamily conversions in the central city, preserving office supply and employment concentration that supports residential demand nearby.

stories.title: “Behind our backs”: Ex-Lt Gov, Florida Rep Carlos Lopez-Cantera, father sued over Miami-Dade land deal
stories.excerpt: Former Florida politician Carlos M. Lopez-Cantera is accused of conspiring with his father to cheat partners out of millions of dollars in a Miami-Dade County real estate deal, a suit says.  The pair face a lawsuit that claims they misled investment partners about the value of an 8.3-acre vacant tract, inducing them to sell it to Quarry Real Estate Holdings, an entity led by the younger Lopez-Cantera, for $40,000 –– even though it potentially could be worth over 400 times more –– with the intention to then flip it, essentially depriving investors from a potentially higher profit, according to the […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami-Dade land fraud suit: Lopez-Cantera family accused of undervaluing 8.3-acre vacant tract.
Property sold at $40k but potentially worth $16M+ (400x valuation gap); investment partnership defrauded.
Sweetwater Pan American West Business Park: vacant land development dispute, fiduciary breach allegation.
stories.summary: Former Florida Lieutenant Governor and U.S. Rep Carlos M. Lopez-Cantera and his father Carlos C. Lopez-Cantera face a lawsuit claiming they conspired to defraud investment partners by misrepresenting the value of an 8.3-acre vacant tract in Sweetwater (Miami-Dade County) within the Pan American West Business Park. The land was sold to Quarry Real Estate Holdings (led by the younger Lopez-Cantera) for $40,000 despite potentially being worth over $16 million, depriving investors of profit upside. The suit includes counts of breach of fiduciary duty and civil conspiracy.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact. This is a real estate fraud/governance dispute affecting a specific land parcel and investor group in Sweetwater, not a market-wide signal. However, it highlights execution and governance risk in land assembly/partnership structures in South Florida, particularly in speculative or early-stage development entities. Developers should monitor Sweetwater (emerging industrial/business park submarket) for land-bank opportunities if properties tied to litigation are eventually liquidated or sold under pressure.

stories.title: LaSalle Acquires Student Housing Complex Near Michigan State University
stories.excerpt: LaSalle Investment Management has acquired Red Cedar Flats, a 190-unit, 436-bed purpose-built student housing community located within walking distance to Michigan State University in East Lansing, Michigan. LaSalle acquired the property on behalf of LaSalle Value Partners IX, alongside joint venture partners King Bridge Partners and Clear Rock Capital. Completed in 2021, Red Cedar Flats is strategically positioned to serve Michigan State University’s student population. The acquisition represents LVP IX’s first investment in the student housing sector.“Student housing is an attractive residential subsector, particularly for newer, campus-adjacent assets at large universities experiencing enrollment growth, limited new development and constrained housing options,” said Jeff Shuster, President of LaSalle Value Partners. Red Cedar Flats is a well-located, high-quality asset that fits this thesis. “The property was acquired below replacement cost with in-place cash flow and represents an attractive opportunity to generate strong risk-adjusted returns for our investors.” Housing operator University Partners will provide property management and co-asset management services. The post LaSalle Acquires Student Housing Complex Near Michigan State University appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: LaSalle Invests Management acquires Red Cedar Flats: 190-unit, 436-bed student housing, East Lansing, MI.
Built 2021, walking distance to Michigan State University; acquired below replacement cost.
LVP IX's first student housing investment; thesis emphasizes enrollment growth + constrained supply.
stories.summary: LaSalle Investment Management, on behalf of LaSalle Value Partners IX alongside King Bridge Partners and Clear Rock Capital, acquired Red Cedar Flats, a 190-unit, 436-bed purpose-built student housing community completed in 2021 and located within walking distance of Michigan State University in East Lansing, Michigan. The acquisition was completed below replacement cost, and LaSalle cited Michigan State's enrollment growth and limited new student housing supply as core investment drivers.
stories.impact: Student housing subsector acceleration. LaSalle's entry into student housing alongside thesis focused on 'newer, campus-adjacent assets at large universities with enrollment growth and constrained housing supply' signals institutional capital redeployment into niche multifamily. For traditional multifamily developers, this indicates: (1) institutional capital now competing in student housing where supply is tight and enrollment growing; (2) campus-adjacent markets in college towns may see M&A consolidation; (3) early-stage development of student housing near major universities facing enrollment pressures remains underserved. Monitor enrollment data and student housing saturation in tier-1 university towns (Michigan State, Ohio State, University of Texas, etc.) for new supply opportunities before institutional capital saturates.

stories.title: Hawkins Way JV Grows NYC Student Housing Portfolio with Upper West Side Sale-Leaseback
stories.excerpt: A joint venture of Hawkins Way Capital and Varde Partners has acquired 117 W. 70th St, a 382-unit student housing property located on Manhattan’s Upper West Side. The $8- million acquisition marks a strategic addition to the JV’s growing urban student housing enterprise, FOUND Study, and further strengthens the firm’s longstanding relationship with the American Musical and Dramatic Academy (AMDA), which will continue occupying and operating the property through a long-term master lease executed concurrently with the sale. THE JV now owns more than 6,000 student housing beds across supply-constrained New York City neighborhoods. The transaction also reflects the FOUND Study platform’s ability to partner with academic institutions with tailored occupancy and operating structures ranging from long-term NNN master leases to fully managed housing solutions. Previously owned and operated by AMDA as a dormitory property, the building will remain occupied by the institution as part of the long-term agreement. The post Hawkins Way JV Grows NYC Student Housing Portfolio with Upper West Side Sale-Leaseback appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Hawkins Way/Varde JV buys 117 W. 70th St, Upper West Side: $80M, 382-unit student housing (sale-leaseback).
AMDA (American Musical and Dramatic Academy) remains occupant via long-term master lease.
JV now owns 6,000+ student housing beds across NYC; platform-driven institutional consolidation.
stories.summary: A joint venture between Hawkins Way Capital and Varde Partners acquired 117 W. 70th Street, a 382-unit student housing property on Manhattan's Upper West Side, for $80 million in a sale-leaseback transaction with the American Musical and Dramatic Academy (AMDA), which continues to occupy and operate the property under a long-term master lease. The acquisition expands the JV's FOUND Study platform to over 6,000 student housing beds across NYC.
stories.impact: Concentrated institutional consolidation of NYC student housing supply via master-lease structures. Hawkins Way/Varde's growing bed count (6,000+ across 'supply-constrained' NYC neighborhoods) reflects: (1) institutional capital capturing student housing as alternative yield in tight multifamily markets; (2) long-term master-lease model (anchor tenant guarantees rent) de-risks institutional entry; (3) limited new construction of student housing in NYC due to cost/zoning, driving acquisition of existing properties. Implications: fewer development opportunities for student housing conversion/new construction in Manhattan; institutional operators will hold beds for long-term NNN cashflow, reducing portfolio turnover and limiting exit windows for smaller developers.

stories.title: Bradford Allen Closes 60K-SF Office Lease in Lincolnshire
stories.excerpt: Bradford Allen has executed a 60,000-square-foot lease with office supply retailer Quill at 300 Tri State International Drive in Lincolnshire, Illinois. The office is within Tri State Lincolnshire, a five-building, 560,000-square-foot office complex owned, operated and leased by Bradford Allen in Chicago’s northern suburbs. Quill is a provider of office equipment, supplies and technology products for businesses. The lease renewal follows the company’s relocation to Tri State Lincolnshire in 2023. Bradford Allen’s Dan Fernitz, executive vice president, Chicago suburbs, negotiated the lease on behalf of ownership. Bradford Allen has invested more than $30 million in improvements since acquiring Tri State Lincolnshire in 2017. Renovations have included substantial upgrades to building lobbies, conference rooms, elevators, the 135-seat auditorium and on-site fitness center, as well as complete replacements to the properties’ roofs and cooling towers. Tri State Lincolnshire offers convenient access to downtown Chicago and O’Hare International Airport. The post Bradford Allen Closes 60K-SF Office Lease in Lincolnshire appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Quill (office supply) renews/expands 60k SF lease at Tri State Lincolnshire (Chicago suburbs).
Bradford Allen owns 560k-SF, 5-building office complex; invested $30M improvements since 2017.
Suburban Chicago office lease renewal amid larger office-to-remote transformation.
stories.summary: Office supply retailer Quill has executed a 60,000-sq-ft lease renewal at 300 Tri State International Drive in Lincolnshire, Illinois, following its 2023 relocation to the property. The building is part of Tri State Lincolnshire, a 560,000-sq-ft, five-building office complex owned and operated by Bradford Allen, which has invested over $30 million in renovations (lobbies, conference rooms, fitness center, roof/HVAC replacements) since acquiring the asset in 2017.
stories.impact: Minimal impact. This is an office lease renewal in suburban Chicago, not a residential or multifamily signal. However, Quill's continued commitment to 60k SF of suburban office space indicates some stabilization in corporate office demand (supply/logistics functions often retain physical footprint). Broader implication: suburban office stabilization may reduce pressure for office-to-multifamily conversion in outer-ring suburbs, preserving office supply. Developers should monitor suburban office vacancy and lease rates to identify when conversion economics become viable.

stories.title: Landmark designation of Bryn Mawr Avenue building stalling demolition, development plans
stories.excerpt: The Commission on Chicago Landmarks could bring to a halt a 95-unit apartment redevelopment with their designation of Bryn Mawr Avenue as a historic landmark. Harlem Irving Companies was planning to demolish 1114 West Bryn Mawr Avenue, 1106 West Bryn Mawr Avenue and 5614 North Winthrop Avenue in order to build a mixed-use apartment complex on the sites. The developer has submitted demolition permit applications, according to Block Club Chicago.  However, the Commission on Chicago Landmarks has given a preliminary landmark recommendation — the first step in designating a place as an officially recognized historic site — to the Bryn […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Chicago Landmarks Commission denies demo permit for 1114 W. Bryn Mawr (1909 Classical Style building).
Harlem Irving Companies' 95-unit apartment redevelopment stalled; already spent $6M; bought project years ago.
Historic designation blocks demolition; Edgewater (Chicago) zoning/entitlement risk materializes.
stories.summary: The Chicago Commission on Landmarks has issued a preliminary landmark recommendation for the Bryn Mawr Avenue corridor (Broadway to Sheridan Road) in Edgewater, blocking demolition of 1114 West Bryn Mawr Avenue, which Harlem Irving Companies intended to raze as part of a 95-unit mixed-use apartment redevelopment. The commission approved demolition of two other sites (1106 W. Bryn Mawr and 5614 N. Winthrop), but denied 1114 W. Bryn Mawr due to its 1909 Classical Style character. Harlem Irving has already invested $6 million in the project since acquisition.
stories.impact: Critical entitlement and execution risk. Landmark designation in Edgewater removes a key parcel from the Harlem Irving redevelopment, materially reducing unit count and project economics (95 units may no longer be achievable). This exemplifies how historic preservation overlay can block infill multifamily development in Chicago's established neighborhoods. Developers underwriting projects in older urban neighborhoods (built pre-1940s) should stress-test for landmark risk during site control and entitlement phase. Harlem Irving's $6M sunk cost now at risk; monitor for project repricing, sale, or redesign to work around the protected building—possible signals of distress or capital recycling.

stories.title: Legacy Real Estate, Urban American push resi plan for Miami Beach-owned land
stories.excerpt: Legacy Real Estate Development and Urban American are seeking a density boost for their multifamily plans on a city-owned property in Miami Beach’s Collins Park neighborhood. The developers’ business entity requested a zoning change to build 105 residential units while preserving portions of the historic Barclay building, which has sat empty for years. The project will also add 1,000 square feet of neighborhood retail.  The proposal is scheduled to go before the Miami Beach Planning Board on July 7 and requires approval from the Miami Beach City Commission to move forward. Miami Beach commissioners selected Legacy Real Estate and Urban […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Legacy Real Estate + Urban American seek zoning increase for Miami Beach Barclay project: 1.6 acres.
Request: 100 → 150 units/acre (bonus up to 180); targets 105 residential units + 1k SF retail (6 stories).
City-owned ground lease; July 7 Planning Board hearing; $2M upfront non-refundable payment.
stories.summary: Legacy Real Estate Development and Urban American, selected in 2024 for long-term ground lease rights from Miami Beach, are seeking a zoning amendment to increase residential density from 100 to 150 dwelling units per acre (with bonus provisions up to 180 du/ac) for their mixed-use redevelopment of the historic Barclay property at 1940 Park Avenue (Collins Park neighborhood). The 1.6-acre project proposes 105 residential units, 1,000 SF of retail, and six-story design; the developer will make a $2 million upfront payment and cover all development costs. The proposal is scheduled for Planning Board review on July 7 and requires City Commission approval.
stories.impact: Miami Beach infill development activity and zoning appetite signal. This is a city-sponsored, ground-lease redevelopment with public sector backing, indicating Miami Beach is actively pursuing residential density in underutilized parcels (Barclay has been vacant). The zoning request (100 → 150 du/ac, bonus to 180) reflects Miami Beach's willingness to grant density bonuses for well-designed projects, particularly on city-owned land. Developers should monitor Miami Beach's zoning initiatives and ground-lease RFP cycles for similar density-grant opportunities; the city appears to be using development partnerships to densify under-producing parcels while maintaining ground ownership and long-term income streams.

stories.title: Terry Black’s BBQ owners plan $63M hotel in San Antonio
stories.excerpt: Some haze cleared this week from one of San Antonio’s smokiest upcoming developments. Black Family Hospitality, founded by the children of Lockhart pitmaster Terry Black, filed plans on Monday to spend $63 million on a three-story hotel and restaurant over a garage and bathhouse at 1411 Alling Street in San Antonio, according to a project filing with the Texas Department of Licensing and Regulation. The hotel will be named the Mandeville, according to the project filing. The plan doesn’t detail the exact size of the hotel, but Terry Black’s BBQ co-owner Mark Black estimated in February that it will have […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Terry Black's BBQ owners (Black Family Hospitality) plan $63M mixed-use development, San Antonio.
3-story hotel (40+ rooms), restaurant, bathhouse ('The Depths'), garage at 1411 Alling Street.
Construction start September 2026, completion June 2028; family acquired 1.4-acre site in 2021.
stories.summary: Black Family Hospitality, founded by children of pitmaster Terry Black, filed plans for a $63 million mixed-use development at 1411 Alling Street in San Antonio near the Pearl District. The project includes a 40+-room hotel ('The Mandeville'), Terry Black's BBQ branch, a non-BBQ restaurant, and a 9,800-sq-ft bathhouse ('The Depths') featuring spas, cold plunges, steam/massage rooms. Construction is slated for September 2026 to June 2028. The family acquired the 1.4-acre site in 2021 and secured city approval in 2024 for initial elements.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. This is a hospitality/mixed-use project (hotel + F&B + spa), not apartment supply. However, the project's location near San Antonio's Pearl District and its lifestyle-amenity-forward positioning reflect broader demand for experiential urban mixed-use development in secondary markets. Developers considering multifamily or mixed-use projects in San Antonio's Pearl area should monitor absorption and leasing velocity on this project; strong hotel/F&B performance would validate the submarket's ability to support premium mixed-use with lifestyle/hospitality components, informing design and phasing of adjacent residential redevelopment.

stories.title: DataBank lands more than $1B boost for Red Oak data center megacampus
stories.excerpt: Dallas-based DataBank, who is developing a 292-acre data center site in Red Oak, announced it is receiving $1.45 billion in new financing. The total amount is split across two separate transactions: an $800 million revolving credit facility, and $650 million to upsize its Red Oak campus. The windfall pushes the data center development to $2.65 billion.  In April, DataBank received a $2 billion construction loan to supercharge the first phase of its campus. The $2 billion figure funds the first three of eight total planned data centers on Stainback Road, the Dallas Morning News reported. In total, the buildings will […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: DataBank secures $1.45B new financing for Red Oak (Dallas) data center megacampus (292 acres).
Total capital stack: $2.65B; earlier $2B construction loan funds 3 of 8 planned data centers.
Phase 1: 600k SF, 180 MW, fully leased; Texas projected 40 GW grid demand by 2028 (4x 2025).
stories.summary: Dallas-based DataBank announced $1.45 billion in new financing ($800 million revolving credit facility + $650 million for Red Oak campus upsizing) for its 292-acre data center megacampus in Red Oak (south of Dallas). Combined with an earlier $2 billion construction loan, total capital stack now reaches $2.65 billion. Phase 1 comprises three data centers (600,000 SF, 180 MW) already fully leased. DataBank has secured $6.15 billion total financing in the past 18 months. Texas is projected to become the nation's largest data center market by 2030, with grid demand reaching 40 GW by 2028 (vs. 8 GW in 2025).
stories.impact: Significant indirect impact on Texas multifamily markets, particularly Dallas and Austin suburbs. DataBank's massive capital deployment and full lease-up of Phase 1 facilities signal: (1) sustained tech infrastructure investment in Texas (data centers are energy-intensive, long-cycle projects requiring multi-year financing); (2) population and employment influx tied to tech operations and supporting services; (3) Red Oak and surrounding I-35 East corridor will attract workers, families, and service-sector populations; (4) grid/infrastructure constraints may limit residential density in high-growth data center zones. Multifamily developers should acquire land in Red Oak, Waxahachie, and nearby I-35 East corridors ahead of data center completion (2027–2028), as worker in-migration will lag facility opening by 12–24 months. This is a macro supply-side signal validating Texas as the nation's data center hub, with ripple effects on residential demand in secondary metros within the state.
